data.description: The following slide deck was published by LVMH Moët Hennessy - Louis Vuitton, Société Européenne in conjunction with this event.

data.title: Royal Caribbean: Over-Feared Slowdown
data.description:
Royal Caribbean Cruises Ltd. (NYSE:RCL) was riding the cruising wave higher before the sudden disruption in travel. The stock is already some $90 off the all-time highs due to fears of a slowdown in travel demand. My investment thesis is ultra-bullish on RCL stock due to the big discount on future growth plans, though a recession would definitely disrupt short-term results.

Travel Slowdown Fears

The big unknown currently impacting the cruise line stocks is how much vacation travel will slow down, if at all. The market is already seeing some signs of weaker business air travel due to the tariff wars, but this type of travel isn't the same as a cruise vacation.

Apollo Asset Management reported weaker daily air travel across the top 10 U.S. airports. However, TSA checkpoint numbers were around 2.7+ million travelers on the key weekend days of Thursday, Friday, and Sunday of the last weekend suggesting a travel slowdown doesn't exist with the numbers matching or topping last April.

The cruise sector hasn't seen the same weak signs yet and a lot of cruises are planned far in advance. Only a few weeks ago, Carnival Corporation & plc (CCL) reported strong cruise demand. The cruise line had 80% of rooms booked for 2025, with record booking levels for trips in 2026.

CCL, the largest cruise line by revenue, reported FQ1 earnings on March 21, so over 3 weeks have passed since this quarterly update on demand. Royal Caribbean will report key Q1 earnings on April 29, with the company starting off the quarter reporting 2025 bookings at load factors in line with prior years at higher rates.

The cruise line even announced plans to launch a premium river cruise vacation concept in 2027. Royal Caribbean committed to ordering 10 ships for the Celebrity River Cruises business in signs of solid growth opportunities ahead.

Perfecta Program

Back shortly after the end of the Covid shutdowns, management introduced the Trifecta program in late 2022. The stock traded around only $55 despite the company targeting a $10 EPS by 2025.

Eventually, the stock soared and recently reached nearly $280. The market probably overreacted to the excitement around the bullish cruise business outlook without a balanced view of normal economic disruptions.

Regardless, Royal Caribbean came out with new financial targets after hitting the previous targets early as follows:

  • 20% compound annual growth rate in Adjusted Earnings per Share compared to 2024.
  • Return on Invested Capital ("ROIC") in the high teens.

The Perfecta program starts with a $11.80 EPS base in 2024. Royal Caribbean would have to hit the following EPS targets to reach 20% annual EPS growth.

  • 2025 - $14.75.
  • 2026 - $18.44.
  • 2027 - $23.05.

The current trade wars could very well disrupt these financial plans with a recession denting vacation demand. Management had targeted ~23% EPS growth this year with a $14.50 EPS target.

The stock has suddenly fallen to only $190 in a sign of how a disconnect exists between the long-term potential of the cruise line and a potential short-term hiccup in vacation demand. Royal Caribbean only trades at 13x consensus EPS targets up at nearly $15 with the corporate target just slightly below analysts.

The consensus analyst estimates only forecast a 2027 EPS target of $20 versus the equivalent of a $23 target to hit the internal Perfecta goals of management for 20% EPS growth. Either way, Royal Caribbean is not priced to reach the much lower consensus targets making the stock an extreme bargain.

The cruise line is in such a better position now that Royal Caribbean recently announced spending $100 million on share buybacks. The company even has a $3 annual dividend with a 1.6% dividend yield due to the far better balance sheet only a few years after restarting operations from the Covid shutdowns.

Royal Caribbean has net debt of nearly $20 billion, but the net PP&E sits up at nearly $32 billion now. The cruise line generated $6 billion in adjusted EBITDA last year.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Royal Caribbean is already pricing in a major slowdown in cruise demand, which may not actually occur. The stock trades at just 13x EPS targets with growth rates targeted at 20% suggesting any downturn in demand is already priced into the stock, though Royal Caribbean can always trade lower in the short term on a feared slowdown.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Delisting Deja Vu: Tariffs Are Putting Alibaba's Valuation At Risk Again
data.description:
Investor angst in Chinese equities is high once again, with the cohort’s early-year rally now proving to be another false dawn. Just when markets thought things couldn't get worse than the Liberation Day reciprocal tariff announcements, the U.S.-China standoff proved otherwise. What started off as a 54% reciprocal tariff on China imports has now become 145% after Beijing's ongoing enforcement of retaliatory measures, which now include a 125% counter-levy on U.S. goods. Beijing’s gone as far as to vow it’ll “fight to the end” on what it calls unilateral aggression from the U.S. The remarks represent a clear inflection point of how Beijing wants to deal with the aggressive U.S. trade war – there’s no backing down – which remains underappreciated.

As the U.S.-China trade war escalates, Alibaba Group Holding Limited (NYSE:BABA) finds itself prone to imminent downside risks beyond its control once again. Despite company-specific strengths found in AI and a business model that’s bound to benefit from Beijing’s recent policy support for innovation and boosting domestic consumption, Alibaba remains exposed in the U.S.-China crossfire. This is evident in the stock’s 30%+ plummet over the past two weeks – what started as potentially a brief stint of profit-taking following the early-year AI rally has taken a steep downturn since the tariff spat began in early April.

While some see opportunity in the wreckage, it’s undoubtedly that Alibaba’s fundamental outlook and underlying risk premium remains exposed to ongoing uncertainties in the U.S.-China tariff war. Despite recent signs of fundamental improvement stemming from Alibaba’s internal reorganization, and favourable policy support from Beijing for its core commerce and AI businesses, the unrelenting tariff pressures remain a significant challenge that can outweigh its strengths.

The unrelenting tariff pressures are likely to become a protracted and significant challenge. This uncertainty represents significant and imminent multiple compression risks that shouldn’t be overshadowed by recent signs of fundamental improvement stemming from Beijing’s favourable policy support for its core commerce and AI business. As a result, we believe the stock could find itself back in the $80-level support range, which would represent a further downside of as much as 20% until the intensifying U.S.-China trade tensions subside.

Don’t Underestimate Trade War Uncertainties

The latest salvo in the U.S.-China trade war has now become a full-blown crisis that sheds significant uncertainties to Alibaba’s outlook due to its international trade exposure. China’s “fight to the end” mentality has not only triggered the U.S. to up the reciprocal tariffs to an unprecedented 145%, but it’s also brought back delisting risks onto the table – something that, we thought, was resolved once and for all after Beijing opted to comply with the Holding Foreign Companies Accountable Act in 2022.

In response to the 145% reciprocal tariffs that went into effect on April 10, Beijing further increased its taxes on U.S. goods from 34% to 84% then 125% as of April 11. And instead of hitting back with another hike to the reciprocal tariffs, U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent’s now announced the prospects of “removing Chinese companies from American stock exchanges”.

The impact of tariffs on Alibaba’s international business is one thing, but uncertainties that come with delisting risks are another.

Assessing the Tariff’s Fundamental Impact

Although Alibaba does not separately disclose its revenues by geography, the company generates at least 12% of its revenues overseas based on total sales reported for its Alibaba International Digital Commerce (“AIDC”) segment alone. Its largest exposure stems from AliExpress – Alibaba’s international e-commerce arm, which management’s been doubling down on in the U.S. in particular with bargain initiatives like AE Choice to fend competition like PDD Holdings Inc.’s (PDD) Temu and Shein.

The latest tariffs and President Trump’s removal of the “de minimis” rule are bound to impact the overseas unit’s growth outlook, which has been a key driver of the AIDC segment’s recent outperformance. Specifically, U.S. consumers have been resonating with the China-buying sensation in recent years, thanks to “cheap thrills” offered across all goods categories, with fast and free shipping perks. The combination of competitive pricing and shipping perks has incentivized the popularity of Chinese-operated e-commerce platforms, spanning AliExpress, Temu and Shein, which have outshone the merely “Made in China” goods offered on others like Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN).

But that’s about to change. Although AliExpress’s U.S. exposure remains in a distant third place compared to its comparable Chinese rivals like PDD’s Temu and Shein, the upcoming changes to the “de minimis” exemption and import tariffs are bound to upend the unit’s growth outlook and earnings profile.

Specifically, the de minimis rule had historically exempted shipments valued at under $800 into the U.S. from tariffs. This relief, which has been critical for overseas e-commerce platforms like AliExpress, is set to be removed beginning May 2. Any shipment that falls below the $800 threshold into the U.S. will now also be subjected to a series of escalated charges. Initially, the new de minimis duty was set at either a 30% tariff or a per-item charge of $25 instead, which will be increased to $50 after June 1. But due to the escalating tariff fallout with China, President Trump's increased the de minimis levy to 120%, or a per-item charge of $100 starting May 1, which will be increased to $200 after June 1. This is expected to upend the unit economics of buying from China from American consumers, and significantly deter purchases moving forward. Even with efforts in stockpiling inventory in U.S. warehouses to bypass the de minimis exemption disruptions, the cost basis of related goods will eventually be upended by the 145% reciprocal tariffs, which, if passed onto consumers, will deter purchases, nonetheless.

As a result, Alibaba’s growth outlook will now depend further on domestic consumption at a time when local economic prospects remain mixed. Admittedly, Alibaba is the most comprehensive e-commerce platform in China. The Taobao platform is known for its wide variety of goods at diverse price points, while Tmall penetrates demand for premium goods that depend on brand confidence. This has made Alibaba’s platforms the preferred destination for local consumers as they look to cash in on the generous trade-in subsidies, which target big-ticket items like consumer electronics and home appliances.

But this is only a drop in the bucket when compared to the broader implications of tariff uncertainties. In addition to the direct impact on AliExpress’ growth and earnings outlook, Alibaba is poised to reel from the indirect impact on the broader Chinese economy as well. Almost a fifth of China’s GDP is dependent on exports. And in 2024, China’s accounted for almost 19% of total U.S. imports, valued at more than $500 billion. With the China economy still reeling from pandemic-era woes and an ongoing property slump, the last thing it needs are the 104% tariffs imposed by the country to which it exports the most to.

Although the trade war might incentivize the central government to further encourage domestic consumption, the stakes are high for consumers. Despite Chinese household savings having hit a record high last year, local consumers remained skeptical about discretionary spending in the face of ongoing property deterioration. The asset class has been a structural component to Chinese wealth creation, hence the destructive impact that the property slump’s had on the local economy in recent years. The impact was also evident in the protracted streak of quarterly deceleration in Alibaba’s core commerce revenues in recent years. And now given the added impact of the U.S.-China tariff fallout, consumers are poised to curtail discretionary spending once again, which threatens to overturn Alibaba’s core commerce recovery and adversely impact its valuation prospects.

Navigating the Unquantifiable from Tariffs

The silver lining is that the fundamental implications of tariffs will eventually be quantifiable. What’s scarier are factors that aren’t quantifiable – and this exists as a byproduct of the ongoing trade war. In response to China’s latest increase in levies on U.S. imports to 84%, the U.S. has brought delisting risks on Chinese stocks from American exchanges back on the table.

The last time this happened was when the Trump administration announced the HFCAA, which, at the time, aimed at targeting Beijing’s refusal to open up the books of U.S.-listed China firms for PCAOB inspection. The double-whammy of delisting risks threatened by the U.S. government, and macroeconomic deterioration and regulatory uncertainties in China had caused Alibaba to plummet 80% in value from its peak before HFCAA compliance was received and any signs of recovery emerged.

A similar setup is now in play. In addition to ongoing macroeconomic uncertainties in China, the added overhang of U.S. delisting risks could throw Alibaba’s valuation back into previous lows. Specifically, the delisting risks are bound to impact Alibaba’s valuation outlook not through its fundamentals, but rather through the risk premium priced into its multiple. To tangibly quantify the impact is almost impossible, as delisting risks harbinger a multitude of implications, with the most adverse being reduced institutional appetite for the stock.

At the peak of delisting fears, and China-related macroeconomic and regulatory woes facing Chinese tech shares in 2022, the cohort – including Alibaba – was deemed “uninvestable” by major funds. This was largely the culprit of ballooning risk premiums for Chinese equities, which drove widening valuation discounts to their U.S. counterparts at the time. Although Alibaba’s fundamentals may have been showing signs of improvement in the face of local policy support for its core commerce and AI business, the risk of this adverse narrative repeating itself shouldn’t be entirely dismissed – especially given the ongoing U.S.-China tariff standoff’s now introducing implications that span beyond quantifiable impacts to fundamentals.

Conclusion

In the worst-case scenario, which is now a reasonable outcome amidst ongoing tariff uncertainties, we believe Alibaba’s valuation could revert to the previous support level found in the $80 range. As discussed in our previous coverages on the stock, the $80 range represents a level at which Alibaba’s downside fundamental prospects can still tangibly support. With previously considered upside catalysts – such as local support for consumption and innovation critical for Alibaba’s core commerce and AI businesses – now taking a back seat, markets may be on the verge of pricing the stock back to its “safety net” levels once again.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: RTX Corporation: A Resilient Aerospace Stock To Buy
data.description:
Recently, I have started adjusting price targets for many stocks. In the semiconductor industry, we see some pressure on earnings estimates. For aerospace stocks, there is also some pressure but less prominent.

For General Electric Company (GE), I recently lowered my price target reflecting a lower market trading multiple. In this report, I will discuss why I believe RTX Corporation (NYSE:RTX) stock remains a buy.

RTX Stock Has Shown Resilience

Since my last report in February, RTX stock has shown resilience gained 2.8% while the market lost 12.6% of its value. The stock price did dip to $117.30, but has bounced around 9% since then. Resilience is definitely one of the things I am looking for in a stock, and RTX seems to have that. That does not mean it is completely isolated from the current market turmoil driven by trade and macroeconomics, but it does make it a somewhat more preferable stock for investors that are looking for stable long-term opportunities.

Global And U.S. Defense Budgets Are Set To Grow

In the U.S., defense stocks have lost momentum on remarks that the current White House administration has been seeking to cut defense budgets. While that is somewhat worrisome, in my view the current global threat environment requires higher defense budgets and not lower budgets. Additionally, I do believe that some cost-cutting can happen at the government in the procurement process with possible cost savings by simplifying the bureaucratic process of procurement and establishing and validating lists of requirements.

We’re now also seeing the first signs of a push for higher defense spending and the U.S. is part of that. The U.S. is now seeking defense spending to be 5% of GDP. It will not happen overnight, but it does show that lower defense spending over the longer term is not the realistic path for NATO members. In Europe, defense spending is already ramping up. This is in part to reduce reliance on the U.S. However, for the time being, for many weapon systems, Europe will still rely on U.S. defense contractors, including RTX.

The recent selection of The Boeing Company (BA) to provide the next-generation stealth air superiority fighter, which will cost around $200 million per unit, is another sign that the U.S. requires higher defense spending. It also seems that President Trump is losing patience with Russia in relation to the peace talks, which are not showing any signs of significant progress and that could result in President Trump doubling down on defense spending.

The push for security autonomy is not going to happen by cutting off the U.S. defense contractors overnight. Realistically, defense budgets will expand, and the European defense contractors will rebuild the industry in parallel with European governments still buying equipment from the U.S. defense industry. I also believe that U.S. defense contractors will carefully assess opportunities to partner with European defense contractors to keep optimal access to the European market.

The opportunity in Europe is big. European leaders have agreed to free up €800 billion over a 4-year period for a temporary increase in the defense budgets. This will pave the way to kickstart an acceleration in defense spending and pave the way to spend more than 3% of GDP on defense by 2030.

The higher defense budgets in Europe will not solely benefit European defense contractors, but could also benefit U.S. defense contractors and could eventually even increase pressure on the U.S. to increase defense budgets more aggressively to remain a leading military power over the long term.

Commercial Aerospace Has A Long-Term Growth Trend

The current trade turmoil and diplomatic stress between the U.S. and its allies leads to a high degree of macroeconomic uncertainty and that may affect demand for commercial products. RTX derived 46% of its sales from commercial aerospace in 2024. So, the company does have some exposure that provides a risk during economic downturns. However, what we generally see is that big economic shocks such as the airline crisis in 2001, the financial crisis in 2008 and the pandemic in 2019 are overcome, and the commercial aerospace industry snaps back to its growth trends.

So, it is a very resilient market in which RTX has positive exposure through its Collins Aerospace business and aero engines. Currently, the Pratt & Whitney geared turbofan engines are providing pressure on RTX due to metal powder issues that have kept hundreds of airplanes grounded, but this should see significant improvement by 2027. Therefore, the company’s current performance is strong but still held back by the GTF issues.

In other words, RTX still has to fully unlock the earnings potential as it works its way through the engine issues and Boeing (unaffected by the GTF issues) and Airbus SE (OTCPK:EADSF) recover production rates. A risk for the business is that continued applications of tariffs may make the globally oriented aerospace supply chain less efficient.

Earnings Estimates Have Revised Downward, But RTX Has Strong Earnings Surprise History

RTX will report its first quarter results on the 22nd of April. Analysts are expecting the company to report revenues of $19.78 billion with core earnings per share of $1.36. Over the past two years, RTX has beaten analyst estimates on EPS every time while missing estimates on EPS only once.

Revenue estimates of $19.78 billion point at 2.5% growth and are revised downward by 1.8% over the past six months and the EPS estimates have come down by 2.2% and indicate 1.6% growth year-on-year.

RTX Stock Gets A Price Target Lift

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For RTX, I am lifting my price target from $145 to $149 and maintaining my buy rating. The increase in the stock price target is supported by the following items:

  • 2.8% higher EBITDA expected for 2025.
  • 1.5% higher EBITDA expected for 2026.
  • A marginal decrease of around 1%-2% in free cash flow for 2025 and 2026.
  • The stock remains undervalued compared to the peer group.
  • Additional $3 billion in available liquidity to return to shareholders by 2027. This has not yet been factored into the stock price calculation.

Overall, I am lifting my price target modestly and also have not implemented a price target for 2027 which is $164.55, and I do believe that in a more predictable market environment, RTX could see its trading multiple increase closer to the peer group. This would unlock additional upside.

The expected dividend yield of 1.9% is not necessarily compelling, but the company has a dividend growth streak of 31 years with an annual growth rate of 6.3% in the past five years. For long-term investors that provides some dividend income that over time could provide a nice yield on cost which I consider to be a superior measure to dividend yields.

Conclusion: RTX Corporation Remains Attractive

There is definitely some turmoil that could affect the defense and commercial end markets. However, I do believe that both end markets are actually growing, and they will more than likely grow over the longer term. So, defense will continue to provide stable growth while commercial aerospace provides somewhat higher growth opportunities through higher airplane production driving sales to original equipment manufacturers with a long-term tail for aftermarket revenues.

So, I remain bullish on the stock noting the end market growth trends and the resilience of RTX stock price.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Bank Of Canada Preview: A Hold Is Slightly More Likely
data.description:
By James Knightley & Francesco Pesole

Tariff exemptions may tilt the balance in favour of a hold

Markets and economists are split as to whether the Bank of Canada will cut interest rates again on 16 April. There is a very slim majority that favours a no-change outcome after 25bp cuts in January and March, but it could go either way.

On the one hand, recent activity data has beaten expectations, and inflation has ticked higher, and the BoC may want to keep some ammunition back, given the US tariff-related uncertainty. However, a weaker jobs report and concerns about future growth, given that 75% of exports go to the US, could justify another cut that would leave the policy rate at 2.50%.

March CPI is released the day before the BoC decision, and we look for the year-on-year inflation rate to rise to 2.7% from 2.6%, with core inflation set to rise to 3%. Throw in the proximity to the election and the tariff carve-outs for USMCA-compliant goods, and we feel it would take a big downside inflation surprise to prompt a rate cut this week. We favour a no change decision, but given the risks to the economy from global trade tensions, we look for 50bp of additional cuts in subsequent months.

CAD still trading on the USD confidence crisis

While we narrowly expect a hold, the 30% implied probability of a cut seems slightly too hawkish. We don’t exclude that markets may modestly increase bets of a cut going into the meeting, although much will depend on the CPI print on Wednesday.

While we expect a positive impact on the Canadian dollar from a hold, the implications for FX of this policy meeting aren’t huge. The outlook for USD/CAD remains much more tied to how far the “sell America” sentiment (i.e. coordinated selloff of Treasuries and equities) will go rather than swings in rate differentials.

The 2-year swap spread has tightened in favour of CAD by only 20bp in April, and at 120bp remains too wide to justify USD/CAD below 1.40. Based on our estimates, the pair is trading 2% below its short-term fair value, which is largely in line with the USD-negative risk premium observed in other crosses.

We think a lot of damage has been dealt to the USD’s safe-haven appeal, and even if trade risk is unwound further, the recovery in USD may only be gradual. We expect 1.40 to work as a gravity line for USD/CAD in the coming weeks, with volatility that may remain elevated for longer.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Arbor Realty: Massive 16% Yield Following Sell-Off
data.description:
Article Thesis

Arbor Realty Trust, Inc. (NYSE:ABR) has seen its share price pull back massively in recent weeks, which has made its valuation decline while its yield has soared from an already high level to an even higher level. While Arbor Realty Trust is not a low-risk investment and a dividend reduction seems quite likely, ABR could nevertheless be a solid investment at current prices for those with a multi-year investment horizon.

Past Coverage

I have written about Arbor Realty Trust several times here on Seeking Alpha, being bullish in some of these articles but being less bullish in others. My most recent article on ABR is from last fall, titled "Arbor Realty: Don't Buy Now". In retrospect, this advice has worked out well, as buying at the time of that article's release would have resulted in a loss of close to 30% to date. With Arbor Realty Trust's valuation being a lot lower now and with the company releasing its FY 2024 earnings since my last article, while the macro picture has changed as well, it is time to take another look at this mortgage REIT.

Arbor Realty's Recent Performance

The mortgage REIT reported its Q4 and full-year earnings results earlier this year. Results were a little worse than expected, with Arbor Realty Trust generating non-GAAP, or adjusted, earnings per share of $0.40 for the fourth quarter. This is still $1.60 annualized, which is quite a lot for a company with a share price of $10.50, but Wall Street analysts were forecasting stronger results -- ABR missed the consensus estimate slightly. On a GAAP basis, profits were weaker, at $0.32 per share, but even that makes for an earnings yield deep in the double-digits considering ABR's current share price.

During the fourth quarter, agency loan originations looked good, at $1.38 billion, which was above the average from Q1-Q3. For the full year, Arbor Realty originated $4.47 billion of agency loans, which makes for a quarterly origination pace of just above $1.1 billion -- Q4 was well above that, suggesting that momentum is positive. Since the Federal Reserve lowered its central bank rate during the second half of 2024, higher originations towards the end of the year could be the result of a more helpful macro backdrop during that period.

Structured loan originations totaled $680 million, which was above the Q1-Q3 average as well -- with the Federal Reserve's interest rate policy being a potential explanation for that. Nevertheless, Arbor Realty's structured loan portfolio shrank throughout 2024, including during the fourth quarter, as the run-off rate was higher than the company's origination rate: Run-off across the structured loan portfolio totaled $900 million during the fourth quarter and close to $2.5 billion during all of 2024. With Arbor Realty Trust's structured loan portfolio shrinking to some degree, that unit's ability to generate profits will decline, all else equal, i.e., assuming unchanged margins, default rates, and so on. Luckily, the headwinds for the structured loans portfolio are balanced out by growth in the agency portfolio, which expanded by 8% during 2024 on a net basis.

Thanks to the growth in Arbor Realty's agency loan portfolio and thanks to strong originations, that unit's revenue expanded by a couple of percentage points during the fourth quarter, relative to one year earlier. The fee-based servicing portfolio expanded from $31 billion at the end of 2024 to $33.5 billion at the end of 2024, which is a compelling growth rate, I believe. Unfortunately, Arbor Realty's fee per dollar it services declined slightly, with the fee margin dropping from 39.0 base points to 37.8 base points over the last year. That is far from a disaster, but I think that keeping an eye on that metric going forward is a good idea -- if the fee margin declines further, that could offset tailwinds from an expanding portfolio and would thus be a hindrance for future profit growth.

In the structured loans portfolio, multifamily bridge loans continue to contribute the vast majority of assets, at 76% of the total, while single-family rental bridge loans contributed another 18%. Mezzanine capital, preferred equity, etc. made up the (relatively small) remainder. The structured loans portfolio declined from $12.6 billion at the end of 2023 to $11.3 billion at the end of 2024, which makes for a decline in the 10% range, driven, as shown above, by financing activity being lower than the portfolio's run-off rate.

If the above trends continue, Arbor Realty Trust will become more and more of an agency loan player, as that part of the business is currently expanding, while the structured loan unit is declining. This is not a bad thing per se, although it does, I believe, take away ABR's unique positioning. If it does indeed become more and more agency loan oriented, it will move closer towards Annaly Capital Management, Inc. (NLY), AGNC Investment Corp. (AGNC), etc.

Arbor Realty Trust's Hefty Dividend

For the most recent quarter, the mortgage REIT declared a dividend of $0.43 per share. This was in line with the previous dividend, so not a huge surprise. With adjusted earnings per share (distributable earnings) coming in at $0.40 for the fourth quarter, the dividend wasn't fully covered -- the payout ratio was north of 100%. In the long run, that doesn't seem very sustainable -- and judging from the very high dividend yield Arbor Realty Trust trades at right now, the market seems to believe that a dividend cut is likely. Throughout all of 2024, Arbor Realty Trust generated distributable earnings of $1.74, which covered the annual dividend of $1.72, but only marginally -- the payout ratio was pretty high, at around 99%.

For the current year, Wall Street analysts are predicting a sizeable earnings decline. GAAP earnings are forecasted at $0.88, and while distributable earnings will most likely be significantly higher, they will, I believe, be lower than the current annualized dividend payout of $1.72 per share. If things remain stable from the fourth quarter, 2025's distributable earnings could come in at around $1.60. Analysts are predicting that GAAP earnings per share will decline by 25% this year -- if distributable earnings were to decline by 25% from 2024 as well (which is, I believe, a rather bearish assumption), the 2025 number could come in around $1.31. I assume that we will land somewhere in between these two, so I think that the result could be in the $1.50 range -- although that is, of course, not guaranteed.

A dividend reduction in 2025 would thus not be a surprise -- and considering that Arbor Realty Trust trades with a dividend yield of more than 16%, the market has seemingly priced in a cut already. The good news is that the dividend yield would still be quite high even if Arbor Realty Trust reduces its dividend, either temporarily or permanently. A 20% dividend reduction would result in a forward dividend yield of around 13%, a 30% dividend reduction would result in a forward dividend yield of around 11.5%, and a 40% dividend reduction would result in a dividend yield of around 9.8%. For someone deploying new money today, none of these scenarios look particularly bad. Even if ABR were to cut its dividend in half -- I believe that is unlikely as profits won't fall off a cliff -- the dividend yield would still be pretty high, at a little north of 8%.

Overall, I think that investors shouldn't see ABR's dividend as sacrosanct -- but even if there is a dividend reduction, the yield will likely remain high, so it wouldn't be a reason to panic sell. Someone deploying money today can assume that the dividend yield would still be quite attractive even if the dividend gets reduced at some point in 2025 (which is not set in stone, of course).

Takeaway

Due to still elevated interest rates and some economic headwinds, the performance of the structured loan business isn't great, but ABR's agency business is doing reasonably well. Profits will likely be lower in 2025, relative to 2024, and that could cause a dividend cut -- but at current prices, that seems to be priced in already.

ABR now trades at a solid ~20% discount to book value, which is a historically cheap valuation -- for value investors, right now could be a good time to enter or expand a position. ABR is now way more attractively priced than it was in the teens, and with the Federal Reserve likely lowering rates at some point in the future, enthusiasm, and business activity could pick up again. For someone with a longer-term view, ABR at a substantial discount to book value could be a nice pick.

data.title: M&T Bank: First Signs Of Some Weakening In Regional Banks
data.description:
We continue coverage of regional bank Q1 earnings season today with M&T Bank Corporation (MTB). As a reminder, this one is one of the larger regional banks that we cover. The stock has corrected heavily with the market and the yield is now 3.5%. However, we saw some declines in key metrics we follow, which keeps us on the sidelines. Let us discuss.

M&T Bank earnings miss in Q1

In M&T Bank's Q1, we saw a sequential decline in deposits on hand as well as total loans. This is a negative. Earnings power was light here, and revenues were up just 0.9% from last year, with M&T Bank bringing in $2.32 billion. This missed consensus by $30 million. Interestingly, we saw a decline from Q4 in the provision for credit losses. The provision for credit losses was down $10 million to $140 million versus $140 million in the sequential quarter, and is down significantly versus $200 million a year ago. We watch provisions because they can weigh on earnings, but also provide insights into loans at risk of default. Non-interest income fell from $657 million to $611 million, but was up from $580 million last year. Net interest income on a tax equivalent basis down slightly from Q4 at $1.71 billion, but up slightly from $1.69 billion a year ago.

With lower net interest income and lower non-interest income, and an overall underwhelming revenue print, M&T Bank saw net income of $584 million, or $3.32 per share. On an adjusted basis, EPS was $3.38m which was below consensus expectations by $0.02. This was a substantial sequential decline versus $3.92 in Q4. So it was a tough quarter, and we did not like the decline in deposits and loans.

Total deposits fell from $164.6 billion to start Q1 down to $161.2 billion in Q1. These deposits are also down from $164.1 billion last year. As we follow this name closely, we have told you that loan growth has stalled. They were down from Q4, but up from a year ago. Total loans were $134.8 billion compared with $135.7 billion in Q4. A year ago, total loans were $133.8 billion. That said, we also know that last year management intentionally tightened standards for loans to help improve quality. More on asset quality in a moment.

We do note one positive, despite net interest income falling from Q4. Net interest margin expanded, and this is a key indicator we follow. Margins were 3.66%. This was up 8 basis points from 3.58% in Q4, and up 14 basis points from 3.52% in the year ago quarter. Overall, margins have been over 3.5% which is positive, and we think the margins bottomed, though we have to keep an eye on the wild moves in rates which could impact margins in the present quarter.

M&T Bank asset quality improves, return metrics soften

Loans were down sequentially, and given the bank has tightened lending standards, we would expect asset quality to be positive. As mentioned, we saw provisions were down slightly from Q4, a slight positive. We also saw loan charge-offs decrease to $114 million, versus $160 million in the linked quarter. This is also down from $138 million last year. The percentage of net charge-offs to total loans improved to 0.34% in Q1 versus 0.47% in the sequential quarter. We also saw lower non-accrual loans this quarter thanks to less commercial real estate, and less commercial/industrial charge-offs. Non-accruals continue to improve. They totaled $1.5 billion at the end of Q1 and this is down $762 million from a year ago, while declining $250 million from the linked quarter. Non-accrual loans were just 1.14% of total loans. The allowance for credit losses was stable at $2.2 billion, or 1.63% of all loans.

M&T Bank is a pretty efficient bank. The efficiency ratio, however, did soften and did return metrics, thanks to lower revenue sources and levels of expense. The efficiency ratio softened from 56.8%, to 60.5%, but was 30 basis points lower from 60.8% a year ago. With the decrease in efficiency from Q4, we saw return metrics decline. The return on average assets was down to 1.14% versus 1.28% in Q4, but is up from 1.01% in the year ago quarter. The return on average tangible equity ticked down to 12.53% vs. 14.66% in Q4 and is also down from 12.67% a year ago.

The decline in return metrics, and the softer than expected margins, led to management trimming its 2025 outlook.

Looking ahead

We still think the bank stock remains a quality hold overall. It is getting close to a buy point on the chart, and with the increase in the dividend yield. We think it is a sector perform, but the one key takeaway was the reduction in net interest income expectations for the year. It was guided previously at $7.1-$7.2 billion, and now the bank sees $7.05 billion to $7.15 billion. A slight decrease. Further it sees less loans being made, now guiding for $135 to $137 billion, versus $137 to $139 previously seen. This comes as the bank also expects lower deposits of $162 to $164 billion, $2 billion lower than expected.

If M&T Bank Corporation's Q2 is weak thanks to rising unemployment and persistently high rates, we could see a further reduction in the outlook. As such, we remain on the sidelines.

data.title: White Mountains: With 2 Catalysts, I Am Upgrading It To A Buy
data.description:
I have written about White Mountains Insurance Group, Ltd. (NYSE:WTM) here, in an introductory piece, and on March 31, in an update following earnings.

In my most recent update, only two weeks back, I rated White Mountains as a hold, indicating that despite a competitive valuation vs peers, I’d wait for the price to fall below book value before initiating a Buy. I anticipated that a shock event would push the price down sooner or later.

Part of my rationale is that WTM has been building an undeployed cash pile, and I wanted to see how they would use cash in their next investment.

As we all know, it has been a wild start to April 2025, with some big price volatility across the entire asset space. It turns out that the shock was sooner rather than later.

The First Catalyst

As the market dropped, WTM dropped as well. The meltdown following the “Liberation Day” tariff announcements sunk most boats. WTM triggered my interest, dropping below book value (albeit marginally).

Now insurance is known as a pretty boring space to invest in, which comes with lower volatility, and I’d expect to see insurers hold up better than the broader market. This proved to be the case for the sector here. When SPY dropped double digits, the iShares U.S. Insurance ETF (IAK) dropped only 6% or so. WTM mirrored IAK. However, WTM missed the bid on the market upswing on April 8. So while IAK is now only down 2%, WTM is still nearly 6% below its mid-March price.

Is there an idiosyncratic rationale for this - something where WTM is exposed more than the average insurance stock? I don’t think so.

  • Clearly no direct tariff exposure.
  • No major insured loss events in April 2025.
  • No earnings news or profit warnings.
  • Nothing too scary in the investment mix.

The item below is the investment mix from the FY 2024 filings.

$2.5bn in bonds. (I checked the counterparty profiles, all IG).

$650m in equities.

$2.1bn in other long-term investments. $1.3bn of which is in portfolio companies, $864m in a mix of alternatives, Lloyds deposit funds, and some hedge fund exposure.

So more equity and alternative market exposure than the average insurer, but nothing alarming.

  • Finally, in the underlying business. Here there is the mainstream Ark/Outrigger P&C underwriter, HG Global, a muni credit underwriter, Kudu, and an insurance specialist investment manager as the scale plays. I can't see too much to be concerned about from a tariff/macroeconomic standpoint to justify an April news-driven discount to the index.

The Second Catalyst

WTM made two new investments in early April.

Broad Street

The Broad Street deal was announced on April 10th. Broad Street is a mid-sized commercial P&C insurance broker based in the US and Canada. Broad Street is a ‘roll up’ model, which buys and operates small independent agencies, allowing the founders to ‘cash out’ of their investments, but keep the business going independently. Roll-up strategies have been a big driver of growth in the insurance brokerage space. The roll-up industry is consolidating fast, and some really large players are emerging. The largest Acrisure, is reportedly preparing an IPO. According to the trade press, Insurance Insider, Acrisure is seeking a valuation of $30bn in its latest funding round. The source is behind a paywall, so I can’t link it here.

White Mountains is part of an investor group with Ethos, BCI, and the Canadian Teachers Pension Fund. Canadian teachers have owned Broad Street since 2012 and will continue to have a large, but undisclosed stake. WTM has invested $150m, a material amount, but modest in their overall business, representing about 2.5% of the whole.

Enterprise Solutions

On April 1, WTM acquired Enterprise Solutions, for an undisclosed sum. Enterprise Solutions is an electrical contractor. At first sight, this seems a poor fit with the insurance company brand. However, this represents a first step in building a strategic position in the insurance value chain end to end.

WTM already owns underwriting companies, brokerage companies, an insurance online sales provider, and specialist insurers. By building a capability in contracting, WTM can exert better control on the cost of repairs (and therefore claims) which is a key success driver in the underwriting business. In an age of higher inflation, controlling claims costs is likely to become increasingly important. Smart move.

Finally, I did a quick cross-check on insider trading. I can see no records of any insider trades in recent weeks.

Conclusion And How I Will Play This

  • As detailed in previous articles, WTM is a high-quality business.
  • They have demonstrated strong capital allocation discipline and played the insurance cycle well.
  • Valuation vs peers is at a discount. WTM is relatively thinly traded and in a niche space.
  • I see the possibility for WTM to close most of the gap to peers, and re-rate to a BV of 1.2x.
  • BV had crept up from 1x to 1.1x in the last year.
  • WTM has made only positive moves in April.
  • I have had WTM as a hold, hoping for a bargain valuation below Book value.
  • WTM has been slow to recover from the tariff tantrum drop with no reason I can identify this.
  • I plan to initiate a small position here to hold for the long term. Will add if the price to book drops further due to market movements.

data.title: China's Challenge To The World Economy
data.description:
Even before President Trump’s escalation of the trade war, China’s economy was in trouble. The bursting of its epic housing and credit market bubble had led to a wave of property developer defaults, falling home prices, and serious strains on local government finances. In turn, that led to a marked slowing in the Chinese economy to among the lowest growth rate in decades. It also resulted in a troublingly high youth unemployment rate and clear signs that the country was on the cusp of price deflation.

In those circumstances, the last thing that the country needed was a negative external shock in the form of punitive import tariffs on its exports to the United States. Those exports constitute close to three percent of its GDP. Yet, that is what China has now got in spades. As part of his effort to rebalance US trading relations with China, Trump now has imposed a 145 percent tariff on all Chinese exports. That alone could shave at least 1 percentage point off China’s GDP. Little wonder then that Goldman Sachs has cut its Chinese economic growth forecast for 2025 to four percent, or well shy of the government’s five percent economic growth target.

Unfortunately, there would seem to be little prospect for an early resolution of the United States-China trade war. In much the same way as for domestic political reasons President Trump has to maintain a tough stance towards China, for domestic political reasons President Xi cannot be seen to be caving in to Trump’s demands. Underlining the point that China will not blink first in its stand-off with America is the way it has responded to the US tariff increases with a 125 percent tariff on US imports of its own, the banning of rare earth metal exports to the United States, and increased restrictions on US companies operating in China.

President Xi also seems to be convinced that China can withstand more economic pain than can Trump ahead of next year’s midterm elections. Trump’s abrupt trade policy U-turn last week in response to stock market and bond market pressure will likely confirm President Xi’s conviction.

The fact that the Chinese housing and credit market bubble over the past decade was greater than that experienced by Japan in the run-up to its lost decade in the 1990s has raised concern that China will now experience a lost economic decade of its own. The start of an all-out trade war with the United States heightens that probability. No longer will China be able to export its way out of its housing and credit market problems through cheapening its currency to boost its export sector. Indeed, far from rescuing the economy, the export sector might itself constitute a further drag on the economy. This would seem to be especially the case if Europe were to join the United States in preventing China from exporting its surplus manufacturing capacity abroad.

All of this has great implications for the rest of the world economy. China has the world’s second-largest economy and, until recently, was the world’s main engine of economic growth. It also was the largest consumer of internationally traded commodities and was the largest external contributor to the S&P 500’s externally generated revenues. A slowing Chinese economy would be particularly bad news for China’s Asian trading partners, the commodity-dependent emerging market economies, and for countries like Australia and Germany for which two countries China is a major export market.

Before turning the screws further on the Chinese economy, the Trump administration might want to think through the implications of a Chinese supply chain disruption for US manufacturers, the effects of high tariffs on domestic inflation, and the spillover effects from China to the rest of the world economy. It might also want to consider the risk that with a troubled domestic economy, President Xi might take a leaf out of Vladimir Putin’s playbook and seek a Taiwan adventure to divert the Chinese population’s attention away from a troubled domestic economy. Such a possibility might concentrate Trump’s mind given that Taiwan currently produces around 90 percent of the world’s most advanced semiconductor chips.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Aristotle Small Cap Equity Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Markets Review

The volatility observed throughout 2024 persisted into the first quarter of 2025. The Russell 2000 Index approached correction territory with a loss of -9.48% for the quarter, following a solid 2024 with the index returning 11.54%. After a moderately positive start to the quarter, February and March were a more challenging market environment due to uncertainty surrounding the precise parameters and implementation of the new administration’s policies, geopolitical tensions and a higher for longer rate environment. Continued economic growth, a stable labor market and firmer inflation data kept the Federal Reserve (Fed) on the more hawkish course they took in December. Chairman Powell held rates steady at the March FOMC meeting and indicated the Fed will maintain a more measured approach going forward, updating their economic projections and forecasts to two rate cuts in 2025.

Stylistically, value stocks outperformed their growth counterparts during the quarter as the Russell 2000 Value Index returned -7.74% compared to the -11.12% return of the Russell 2000 Growth index. This is a reversal from last year where growth significantly outperformed value.

From a factor performance perspective, the quarter saw a change in market preference to higher-quality companies, favoring dividend-paying, defensive stocks. This change in sentiment was due in part to level interest rates, moderating US economic activity, and an increase in recession risk.

At the sector level, defensive sectors outperformed cyclical sectors, with Utilities (+5.28%), a sector often perceived as a bond proxy, being the only sector in the Russell 2000 Index to post a positive return during the quarter. The worst performing sectors were Information Technology (-18.39%), Consumer Discretionary (-14.91%), Energy (-12.95%), Communications (-12.51%) and Industrials (-11.03%) while Utilities (+5.28%), Health Care (-8.29%), Materials (-6.96%), Financials (-4.35%), Real Estate (-3.28%) and Consumer Staples (-0.07%) performed best.

Performance Review

For the first quarter of 2025, the Aristotle Small Cap Equity Composite posted a total return of -7.34% net of fees (-7.20% gross of fees), beating the -9.48% total return of the Russell 2000 Index. Outperformance was driven by strong security selection in the Information Technology, Industrials and Materials sectors coupled with an underweight allocation to Consumer Discretionary. Security selection in Consumer Discretionary, Consumer Staples and Energy coupled with an overweight allocation to Information Technology detracted.

Relative Contributors Relative Detractors
Alamos Gold (AGI) Columbus McKinnon (CMCO)
Huron Consulting Group (HURN) MACOM Technology Solutions
MACOM Technology Solutions (MTSI) Wolverine World Wide (WWW)
AerCap Holdings (AER) ASGN Incorporated (ASGN)
Merit Medical Systems (MMSI) Knowles Corp (KN)

CONTRIBUTORS

Alamos Gold (AGI-CA), engages in the exploration, development, mining and extraction of precious metals. The company benefited from rising gold prices as investors purchased the commodity as an inflation hedge. We maintain our investment as we believe in the company’s lower geopolitical risk profile, solid production growth plan from 600k oz to 1M oz per year over the next five years, and strong operational track record.

Huron Consulting Group (HURN), a specialty consulting company that provides financial, operational, and digital consulting services to health care, education and commercial clients, appreciated after delivering strong results highlighted by continued momentum within the company’s health care and commercial segments, more than offsetting the education segment. We maintain our investment, as we believe the company remains well-positioned to capitalize on a demand backdrop aided by financial and operational pressures in its largest end-markets, along with secular tailwinds supporting digital transformation, analytics and cloud consulting.

DETRACTORS

Columbus McKinnon (CMCO), engages in the design, manufacture, and marketing of material handling products and systems. The company came under pressure following the announcement of its intent to acquire Kito Crosby, a global provider of lifting solutions and securement. Investor concern about the size and financing of the deal contributed to the weakness. We continue to maintain a position, for now, as we continue to evaluate the risk/reward potential associated with the deal.

MACOM Technology Solutions (MTSI), a designer and manufacturer of high-performance semiconductor products, declined along with the broader semiconductor industry during the quarter. We maintain our position, as we believe the company’s meaningful exposure to growing demand from Data Center and 5G end market applications along with the integration of recent acquisitions should drive additional shareholder value in periods to come.

Recent Portfolio Activity

Buys/Acquisitions Sells/Liquidations
Agree Realty Corporation (ADC) Avid Bioservices
Old National Bancorp (ONB) Barnes Group
Valvoline (VVV) Designer Brands (DBI)
Veeco Instruments (VECO) Flushing Financial (FFIC)
ModivCare (MODV)
Summit Materials

BUYS/ACQUISITIONS

Agree Realty Corporation (ADC), is a real estate investment trust that owns, manages and develops primarily neighborhood community shopping centers and single tenant properties leased to major retail tenants such as Sherwin-Williams, Wal-Mart and TJX Companies. We believe the company’s high-quality portfolio, recession resistant retail categories and strong balance sheet coupled with management’s proactive tenant management and acquisition pipeline will benefit the company on a go-forward basis.

Old National Bancorp (ONB), is a regional bank serving clients primarily in the Midwest and Southeastern U.S. We believe the company’s geographic location, strong balance sheet, increased loan growth and repricing of fixed-rate loans to higher rates will benefit NIMs on a go-forward basis.

Valvoline (VVV), is a pure-play auto quick lube and maintenance service provider with over 2,000 locations in the U.S. and Canada. The company provides quick and convenient stay-in-your-car automotive preventative maintenance through its full-service oil changes from skilled technicians in less than 15minutes. We believe the company will benefit from increased market share gains, driven by both corporate and franchise expansion, growing its scale, accelerating growth and increasing its moat.

Veeco Instruments (VECO), engages in the development, manufacture, sale, and support of semiconductor process equipment. Its technologies consist of metal organic chemical vapor deposition, advanced packaging lithography, wet etch and clean, laser annealing, ion beam, molecular beam epitaxy, wafer inspection, and atomic layer deposition systems. The company should benefit from the evolution of the semiconductor chip as manufacturers seek new technologies to enhance the manufacturing process that will enable them to produce smaller and faster chips.

SELLS/LIQUIDATIONS

Avid Bioservices (CDMO), is a dedicated biologics Contract Development and Manufacturing Organization working to improve patient lives by providing high quality development and manufacturing services to biotechnology and pharmaceutical companies. The company was acquired and taken private by GHO Capital and Ampersand Capital Partners.

Barnes Group (B), is a globally recognized industrial and aerospace manufacturer and service provider that stands out for its highly engineered products, differentiated industrial technologies, and innovative solutions. The company was acquired and taken private by Apollo Funds.

Designer Brands (DBI), is a North American company which engages in the design, production, and retail of footwear and accessory brands. We sold the position due to deteriorating corporate fundamentals.

Flushing Financial (FFIC), a NY-based regional bank holding company, was sold because the shares had reached our valuation target.

ModivCare (MODV), is a Colorado-based healthcare services company that provides non-emergency medical transportation (NEMT), homecare services, and remote patient monitoring to Medicaid and Medicare populations. We sold the position due to a deteriorating fundamental outlook that resulted in a deteriorating financial position.

Summit Materials (SUM), is a North American supplier of aggregates, cement, and ready-mix concrete for the construction industry. The company was acquired by Quikrete Holdings, Inc.

Outlook

We continue to remain optimistic about the long-term potential for the small-cap segment of the U.S. market. Valuations remain compelling relative to large caps, with the Russell 2000 Index trading near multi-decade lows on a relative basis. Potential tailwinds, including deregulation, lower corporate tax rate, increased M&A activity, continued reshoring of U.S. manufacturing, and infrastructure-related spending, could provide additional support for small-cap stocks. There may be some short-term volatility as the new administration’s policies are implemented and absorbed by markets. We also remain mindful of risks such as inflation reaccelerating, increased geopolitical tensions, and potential U.S. economic weakness.

Positioning

Our current positioning is a function of our bottom-up security selection process and our ability to identify what we view as attractive investment candidates, regardless of economic sector definitions. Overweights in Industrials and Information Technology are mostly a function of our underlying company specific views rather than any top-down predictions for each sector. Conversely, we continue to be underweight in Consumer Discretionary, as we have been unable to identify what we consider to be compelling long-term opportunities that fit our discipline given the rising risk profiles of many retail businesses and a potential deceleration in goods spending following a period of strength. While the portfolio’s allocation to Health Care is modestly below that of the benchmark, we continue to remain underweight the Biotechnology industry as many companies within that group do not fit our discipline due to their elevated levels of binary risk. Meanwhile we have found more opportunities for steady company growth within the Provider, Services and Equipment industry group so more of our exposure is to that area of Health Care. Given our focus on long-term business fundamentals, patient investment approach and low portfolio turnover, the strategy’s sector positioning generally does not change significantly from quarter to quarter. However, we may take advantage of periods of volatility by adding selectively to certain companies when appropriate.

Disclosures

The opinions expressed herein are those of Aristotle Capital Boston, LLC (Aristotle Boston) and are subject to change without notice.

Past performance is not indicative of future results. The information provided in this report should not be considered financial advice or a recommendation to purchase or sell any particular security. There is no assurance that any securities discussed herein will remain in an account’s portfolio at the time you receive this report or that securities sold have not been repurchased. The securities discussed may not represent an account’s entire portfolio and, in the aggregate, may represent only a small percentage of an account’s portfolio holdings. The performance attribution presented is of a representative account from Aristotle Boston’s Small Cap Equity Composite. The representative account is a discretionary client account which was chosen to most closely reflect the investment style of the strategy. The criteria used for representative account selection is based on the account’s period of time under management and its similarity of holdings in relation to the strategy. It should not be assumed that any of the securities transactions, holdings or sectors discussed were or will be profitable, or that the investment recommendations or decisions Aristotle Boston makes in the future will be profitable or equal the performance of the securities discussed herein. Aristotle Boston reserves the right to modify its current investment strategies and techniques based on changing market dynamics or client needs. Recommendations made in the last 12 months are available upon request.

Returns are presented gross and net of investment advisory fees and include the reinvestment of all income. Gross returns will be reduced by fees and other expenses that may be incurred in the management of the account. Net returns are presented net of actual investment advisory fees and after the deduction of all trading expenses.

Effective January 1, 2022, the Aristotle Small Cap Equity Composite has been redefined to exclude accounts with meaningful industry-specific restrictions or substantial values-based screens hampering implementation of the small cap strategy.

All investments carry a certain degree of risk, including the possible loss of principal. Investments are also subject to political, market, currency and regulatory risks or economic developments. International investments involve special risks that may in particular cause a loss in principal, including currency fluctuation, lower liquidity, different accounting methods and economic and political systems, and higher transaction costs.

These risks typically are greater in emerging markets. Securities of small‐ and medium‐sized companies tend to have a shorter history of operations, be more volatile and less liquid. Value stocks can perform differently from the market as a whole and other types of stocks. The material is provided for informational and/or educational purposes only and is not intended to be and should not be construed as investment, legal or tax advice and/or a legal opinion. Investors should consult their financial and tax adviser before making investments.

The opinions referenced are as of the date of publication, may be modified due to changes in the market or economic conditions, and may not necessarily come to pass.

Aristotle Capital Boston, LLC is an independent investment adviser registered under the Investment Advisers Act of 1940, as amended. Registration does not imply a certain level of skill or training. More information about Aristotle Boston, including our investment strategies, fees and objectives, can be found in our Form ADV Part 2, which is available upon request. ACB-2504-19

Performance Disclosures

Sources: CAPS Composite Hub, Russell Investments

Composite returns for periods ended March 31, 2025, are preliminary pending final account reconciliation.

*The Aristotle Small Cap Equity Composite has an inception date of November 1, 2006, at a predecessor firm. During this time, Jack McPherson and Dave Adams had primary responsibility for managing the strategy. Performance starting January 1, 2015, was achieved at Aristotle Boston.

**For the period November 2006 through December 2006.

Past performance is not indicative of future results. Performance results for periods greater than one year have been annualized.

Effective January 1, 2022, the Aristotle Small Cap Equity Composite has been redefined to exclude accounts with meaningful industry-specific restrictions or substantial values-based screens hampering implementation of the small cap strategy.

Returns are presented gross and net of investment advisory fees and include the reinvestment of all income. Gross returns will be reduced by fees and other expenses that may be incurred in the management of the account. Net returns are presented net of actual investment advisory fees and after the deduction of all trading expenses. Please see important disclosures enclosed within this document.

Index Disclosures

The Russell 2000® Index measures the performance of the small cap segment of the U.S. equity universe. The Russell 2000 Index is a subset of the Russell 3000® Index representing approximately 10% of the total market capitalization of that index. It includes approximately 2000 of the smallest securities based on a combination of their market cap and current index membership. The Russell 2000 Growth® Index measures the performance of the small cap companies located in the United States that also exhibit a growth probability. The Russell 2000 Value® Index measures the performance of the small cap companies located in the United States that also exhibit a value probability. The volatility (beta) of the composite may be greater or less than the benchmarks. It is not possible to invest directly in these indices.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
